title: Why is it so hard to build big energy projects? | Wood Mackenzie
gist: In an Energy Gang episode, Bechtel President Craig Albert argues US energy infrastructure is throttled by slow, fragmented permitting, project uncertainty, and a neglected skilled-trades workforce, citing lessons from the Vogtle AP1000 reactors and the well-executed US LNG industry as templates for building faster to meet data-center, AI, and decarbonization demand.

insights.claim: US energy infrastructure delays stem from a slow, fragmented, and uncertain approval process that pushes up costs, delays revenue, creates financing risk, and slows progress toward energy security and decarbonization.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Permitting reform is the highest-leverage fix for US infrastructure delivery; project timelines and financing costs hinge on it.
insights.quote: the process used to take all those factors into account when approving large projects is slow, fragmented and uncertain

insights.claim: Bechtel's president cites Australia's Western Sydney International Airport moving from site selection to full construction approval in just two and a half years, versus eight to ten years for the same process in the US.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A 3-4x approval-time gap versus peers quantifies the US competitive disadvantage in infrastructure delivery.
insights.quote: which moved from site selection to full construction approval in just two and a half years. In the US, he says, the same process could easily take eight to ten
insights.locator: Craig cites the Western Sydney International Airport project

insights.claim: Once a project is assessed and approved, preventing it from being endlessly revisited requires trust and certainty in decision-making, which the US system particularly lacks.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Regulatory finality is a distinct lever from initial approval speed; litigation risk deters capital.
insights.locator: The conversation digs into the role of trust and certainty

insights.claim: The two new AP1000 reactors at the Vogtle plant in Georgia were the first greenfield US nuclear project in decades, and the biggest takeaway was the importance of deeply integrated EPC where engineering, procurement and construction work as one system from the start.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Integrated EPC delivery is the operational template for the coming nuclear buildout; fragmented delivery drives overruns.
insights.quote: His biggest takeaway is the importance of deeply integrated EPC: engineering, procurement and construction working as one system from the start.
insights.locator: The discussion then turns to nuclear power

insights.claim: Successful nuclear delivery also requires earlier investment in workforce training, stronger supply-chain visibility, and better sequencing to reduce costly reworks.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Front-loading workforce and supply-chain planning is the differentiator between on-budget and over-budget nuclear.
insights.locator: He also stresses the need for earlier investment

themes.theme: LNG and solar as delivery models

insights.claim: The US LNG industry offers a hopeful delivery model because construction companies build repeated, standardised projects with integrated delivery and relentless bottleneck-fixing, avoiding huge cost overruns and multi-year delays.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Standardization and repetition, not bespoke megaprojects, is the path to predictable infrastructure delivery.
insights.quote: In that industry the construction companies are building repeated, standardised projects, with integrated delivery, and a relentless focus on finding and fixing bottlenecks.
insights.locator: Craig explains why the US LNG industry

insights.claim: Bechtel has delivered dozens of LNG trains all on schedule and within budget while still improving speed from one project to the next.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Demonstrated on-time LNG delivery validates the standardized-repeatable approach as transferable to other sectors.
insights.quote: Bechtel has delivered dozens of LNG trains, he says, all on schedule and within budget, while still improving speed from one project to the next.
insights.locator: Craig explains why the US LNG industry

insights.claim: Similar lessons apply in solar, where scale, automation and better execution are helping push installation rates sharply higher.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Automation and execution improvements are accelerating solar deployment, a positive signal for solar buildout pace.

insights.claim: Skilled craft labour is the other great constraint on US building capacity, since every new power plant, grid upgrade, semiconductor fab and airport depends on it, and restoring the status of skilled trades is essential to the country's ability to build.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Labour scarcity is a binding constraint across power, grid, chips and data centers; treat trades as a strategic asset.
insights.quote: Restoring the status of skilled trades is not just a cultural issue. It is essential to the country's ability to build
insights.locator: Craig argues that workforce is the other great constraint

insights.claim: If the US wants more energy security, more electricity for AI and data centres, and faster decarbonisation, it must fix permitting, reduce project uncertainty, invest earlier in supply chains, and treat skilled labour as a strategic asset.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Consolidates the four levers investors should watch to gauge whether US infrastructure delivery can meet AI power demand.
insights.quote: if the US wants more energy security, more electricity for AI and data centres, and faster progress on decarbonisation, it has to get much better at building
insights.locator: The closing message is both practical and cautionary
